persons.arguments_against: The strongest case against: the stint runs to 1914, when a man born 1823 would be 91, and the Aikins family produced a second prominent official (Sir James A. M. Aikins, later himself Lieutenant-Governor of Manitoba) — so the late rows could belong to a son, making the cluster a conflation. This fails: every row prints 'J. C. Aikins' or the full 'James Cox Aikins', never the son's initials J. A. M., and the late rows sit under the 'Lieutenant-Governors' roster department — the Colonial Office List's practice of listing past office-holders, which the dossier instructions flag ('honoured retirees stay listed'); they do not assert current service. The early rows match the biography step for step with full forenames: 'James Cox Aikins / Senator / Senate of Canada' (bio: called to the Senate 1867), 'Secretary of State for Canada' from the 1879 edition (bio: 're-appointed to same office in Sir John's new administration, 19th Oct., 1878'), 'Secretary of State and Registrar-General / Privy Council' in 1880 (verbatim office match), and 'Lieutenant Governor of Manitoba' from 1883 (bio: 'appointed lieutenant governor of Manitoba, 15th October, 1882'). With only 6 Aikinses in the corpus and a distinctive double forename, no namesake hypothesis survives.

evidence_cited.fact: re-appointed to same office in Sir John's new administration, 19th Oct., 1878
appointed lieutenant governor of Manitoba, 15th October, 1882

persons.would_overturn: Evidence that the post-1906 'J. C. Aikins' roster rows refer to a different person with the same initials in the Lieutenant-Governors list, which would warrant splitting the stint's tail rather than reassigning it.

notes: Full double-forename match plus a verbatim office-sequence match across three distinct offices (Senator, Secretary of State and Registrar-General, Lieutenant-Governor of Manitoba) with dates agreeing to the year. The 1905-1914 rows are retrospective Lieutenant-Governors roster listings, a known COL convention, and do not imply service at age 90+.
